summary: Matched fighter function `func_8007C114` by replacing the MWERKS inline assembly implementation and non-GEKKO `NOT_IMPLEMENTED` stub with C. The function reads the fighter from `fighter_gobj->user_data`, checks the held item at `fp->x1974_heldItem`, calls `func_800C555C(fighter_gobj)` only when the held item kind is `It_Kind_Hammer`, then clears `fp->x2219_flag.bits.b4`. The PR also moved the `func_800C555C` prototype into `ft_unknown_006.h` and included that header.
what_changed: Removed the inline assembly body for `func_8007C114` under `MWERKS_GEKKO`.
Replaced the non-GEKKO `NOT_IMPLEMENTED` body of `func_8007C114` with a matching C implementation.
Added `#include <melee/ft/ft_unknown_006.h>` to access the declaration for `func_800C555C`.
Kept the local float constants `lbl_804D8320` and `lbl_804D8324` guarded by `#ifdef MWERKS_GEKKO`, while ending that guard before the C function.
Preserved the forward declaration `void func_8007C224(HSD_GObj* fighter_gobj);` in `code_8007C114.c`.
smart_moves: Translated the assembly control flow directly into a small C sequence: null-check held item, inspect item kind, conditionally call the hammer handler, then clear the fighter flag.
Used the existing struct fields `fighter_gobj->user_data`, `fp->x1974_heldItem`, and `fp->x2219_flag.bits.b4` instead of offset-based placeholder access.
Matched the item-kind comparison against the named enum/value `It_Kind_Hammer` rather than leaving the raw assembly immediate `0x1c`.
Promoted `func_800C555C` from an inline `extern unk_t func_800C555C();` in the C file to a typed header declaration taking `HSD_GObj*`.
Used a `switch` on `itGetKind(...)` even for the single handled case, mirroring a pattern that may match compiler output for item-kind dispatch better than a simple `if`.
decomp_lessons: When matching small fighter helpers, start from the assembly offsets and map them to existing `Fighter` fields; here `0x1974` corresponded to `x1974_heldItem` and byte/bit access at `0x2219` corresponded to `x2219_flag.bits.b4`.
Conditional item-specific behavior can often be made clearer and more match-friendly by combining `itGetKind` with named item constants such as `It_Kind_Hammer`.
If a matched function calls an existing unknown function, add the prototype to the appropriate fighter header instead of using a local untyped `extern`.
Clearing a bitfield member such as `fp->x2219_flag.bits.b4 = 0` can replace an `rlwimi`-style assembly bit clear when the struct bit layout is already known.
Removing `NOT_IMPLEMENTED` and inline assembly together is a common milestone: it makes the function portable in non-GEKKO builds while preserving matching under MWERKS.
naming_conventions: `func_8007C114` remained unnamed, indicating there was not enough evidence in this PR to assign a semantic name.
`func_800C555C` also remained unnamed but was declared with an explicit `HSD_GObj*` parameter.
Existing project field names were reused: `x1974_heldItem`, `x2219_flag`, and `bits.b4`.
The item kind was expressed with the named constant `It_Kind_Hammer` instead of the raw value `0x1c`.
The fighter object parameter name `fighter_gobj` was used consistently for `HSD_GObj*` fighter object inputs.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Converted the assembly prologue/body/epilogue for `func_8007C114` into C and removed the `asm` block.
Mapped the null check on `lwz r3, 0x1974(r31)` to `if (fp->x1974_heldItem != NULL)`.
Mapped `itGetKind` comparison against `0x1c` to a `switch` case for `It_Kind_Hammer`.
Mapped the conditional branch to `func_800C555C` into a `case It_Kind_Hammer:` call followed by `break`.
Mapped the `rlwimi`-based bit clear at byte offset `0x2219` to `fp->x2219_flag.bits.b4 = 0`.
Kept MWERKS-only local float constants guarded separately from the now-shared C implementation.
follow_up_queries: Look for later PRs that rename or further document `func_8007C114`.
Look for later PRs that rename or type `func_800C555C` more semantically.
Search for other uses of `x2219_flag.bits.b4` to infer the meaning of this fighter flag.
Search for other item-specific fighter logic using `itGetKind` and `It_Kind_Hammer` for matching patterns.
